paragraphs.text: IN TWO PREVIOUS ISSUES OF
THE MASTERKEY
Vol. 52, Nos. 1 & 4, 1978) several exciting discoveries of ethnographic notes pertaining to the "Lone Woman" of San Nicolas Island were reported.

These notes were found among the extensive papers of John P. Harrington for the Ventureño Chumash. Recently, however, two additional notes on the "Lone Woman" have surfaced among Harrington's papers for the Barbareño Chumash. Interviewed in Santa Barbara some time between 1912 and 1922, the informant in this case was Luisa Ygnacio, a full-blood Barbareño Chumash who was baptized at Mission Santa Barbara about 1830. Harrington used the abbreviation[s] "Sa." and "La." when referring to her (Blackburn 1975: 19).
The first short note is as follows:
Sa. has never seen a shell fishhook, except the one that the last woman of San Nicolas had
. It was a big shell fishhook, and [the] fishline [with it] was as thick as a lead pencil. Sa.'s spouse brought it.1 Sa. had it at Sa.'s home on the ranch, but after a while it got thrown away.
Sa. also had at the ranch two feather capes belonging to the last woman of San Nicolas which Sa.'s spouse brought at the same time that he brought the fishhook.

2 These were small (not big) and were made by sewing together seagull skins with [the] feathers still on, Sa. thinks. [The] feathers [were] on [the] outside [of the capes]. Used them [by wearing them] thrown over [the] shoulders. When Sa.'s spouse died, Sa. threw the capes and hook away.3"
The second note mentions these details concerning the woman:
They had the last woman of San Nicolas Island here in Santa Barbara.

Sa. did not go to see her, but many people did. Martina went to see her, and either Martina or others said she talked like a Fernandina.4 So they got Pilar to come and try to talk with her.5 Pilar talked good Barbareño Chumash, but she never forgot her native tongue and talked it when the Fernandinos came here to Santa Barbara. Pilar tried to talk with the woman in Fernandina, but she said she could not understand.6

"If they had let the woman stay with the Indians here, she would have been all right. But they kept her with Qorqe (George Nidever). Martina and Pilar came from Ventura one time and got some clams and maybe some other things on the road, and when they passed by here, in Santa Barbara, they visited the woman and gave her the clams. The woman was greatly pleased. Martina and Pilar had just made a little trip to Ventura. Sa. thinks they had seen the woman before that time."
BLACKBURN THOMAS
December's Child: A Book of Chumash Oral Narratives. University of California Press. Berkeley.
DITTMAN, CARL

Narrative Of a Seafaring Life on the Coast of California. Reprinted in Original Accounts of the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island. ed. by Robert Heizer & Albert Elsasser, pp. 1-7. Ballena Press. Ramona.
HUDSON, TRAVIS
Chumash Canoes of Mission Santa Barbara: the Revolt of 1824. Journal of California Anthropology. Vol. 3, No. 2: 5-15. Malki Museum Press. Banning.
Some J.P. Harrington Notes on the "Lone Woman" of San Nicolas. The Masterkey, Vol. 52, No. 1: 23-28. Southwest Museum. Los Angeles.
An Additional Harrington Note on the "Lone Woman" of San Nicolas. The Masterkey. Vol. 52, no 4: 151-154. Southwest Museum. Los Angeles.
Breath of the Sun: Life in Early California as Told by a Chumash Indian, Fernando Librado, to John P. Harrington. Matki Museum Press. Banning.
A Rare Account of Gabrielino Shamanism from the Notes of John P. Harrington. Journal of California and Great Basin Anthropology. Vol. 1, No. 2: 356-362. Mallti Museum Press. Banning.
NIDEVER, GEORGE
The Life and Adventures of a Pioneer of California since 1834. pp. 7-15 in Original Accounts (see Dittman, above).
